proposals.title: Invariant Risk Minimization Beyond Linear Probes: Theoretical Guarantees for Nonlinear Classifiers
proposals.motivation: Invariant Risk Minimization (IRM) is a promising framework for out-of-distribution generalization by learning invariant causal predictors. However, its theoretical guarantees are largely restricted to linear classifiers atop the learned representation. In practice, deep learning employs powerful nonlinear classifiers, creating a critical theory-practice gap. Understanding the conditions under which IRM's invariance principle generalizes to nonlinear predictors is essential for its reliable application in complex, real-world settings with distribution shift.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that, under specific geometric and complexity constraints on the data manifold and function class, the invariance principle of IRM can be provably extended to broad families of nonlinear classifiers, ensuring robustness across training environments.
proposals.method: We will analyze the IRM objective where the invariant predictor is a composition of a representation map and a nonlinear classifier from a parameterized family (e.g., shallow neural networks). Using tools from algorithmic stability and Rademacher complexity, we will derive generalization bounds that depend on the complexity of the classifier and the separation of spurious features in the representation space. We will complement the theory with controlled experiments on synthetic data with known geometric structure, measuring the violation of invariance as classifier complexity increases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first theoretical framework for IRM with nonlinear predictors, formally delineating when invariance can be expected to hold. It will yield practical guidelines for choosing classifier architectures in IRM-based systems and bridge a fundamental gap between the algorithm's theory and its common implementations.
proposals.novelty: Existing IRM theory focuses on linear probes; extending guarantees to nonlinear classifiers requires analyzing the interaction between representation geometry and predictor complexity, a non-trivial step beyond current analyses. This directly addresses a core limitation noted in the original IRM work and its subsequent critiques.

proposals.title: Invariant Risk Minimization for Meta-Test Generalization in Few-Shot Learning
proposals.motivation: Few-shot learning (FSL) aims to adapt to novel tasks with minimal data, but standard meta-learning algorithms often fail when the test task distribution differs from the meta-training environments, a common real-world scenario. The Invariant Risk Minimization (IRM) principle, which seeks predictors invariant across training environments, offers a promising framework for robust generalization, yet its application has been limited to standard i.i.d. settings with abundant per-environment data. This work investigates whether the IRM objective can be adapted to the extreme data scarcity of the FSL regime to combat distributional shift at meta-test time.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that reformulating the meta-learning objective to explicitly encourage invariant feature representations across the few-shot support sets of meta-training tasks will yield models that generalize more robustly to out-of-distribution (OoD) novel tasks during meta-testing.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel meta-learning algorithm that integrates the IRMv1 penalty into episodic training. For each meta-batch, we will treat the support sets of multiple sampled tasks as distinct 'environments.' The model will be optimized to minimize a joint loss: the standard few-shot loss (e.g., prototypical loss) plus a regularization term enforcing that the optimal classifier head for the features is consistent (approximately invariant) across these task environments. We will implement this within a memory-augmented or transformer-based meta-learner, drawing inspiration from the 'Birth of a Transformer' memory viewpoint, to manage the complexity of the invariance constraint with limited data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first principled framework and practical algorithm for applying IRM in data-scarce, multi-task settings. It will deliver new benchmarks for OoD generalization in few-shot learning and offer theoretical insights into the interplay between invariance and sample efficiency. The resulting method aims to significantly improve the reliability of FSL systems under covariate shift.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in scaling down the IRM framework—which typically requires multiple large datasets—to operate effectively within the few-shot episodic paradigm, where each 'environment' contains only a handful of examples, a non-trivial adaptation not addressed in prior IRM or meta-learning literature.

proposals.title: Consensus-Optimized Multi-Agent Learning for Emergent Linguistic Representations
proposals.motivation: Current NLP systems often treat tasks like summarization, QA, and dialogue as isolated problems, leading to models that lack robust, generalizable linguistic understanding. Multi-task learning offers some benefits but typically relies on a single model or simple parameter sharing, which can lead to negative interference and fails to formalize the interaction between specialized learners. A principled framework is needed to coordinate diverse, task-specialized agents to discover a shared representation space that captures transferable knowledge without catastrophic forgetting.
proposals.hypothesis: Formalizing the co-training of multiple, architecturally diverse NLP agents via consensus optimization will lead to a globally stable equilibrium where the emergent shared representations exhibit superior generalization and robustness to distributional shift across tasks compared to standard multi-task or single-agent paradigms.
proposals.method: We will instantiate a set of agent models, each a Transformer-based architecture (e.g., encoder-only, encoder-decoder) specialized for a distinct NLP task and dataset. Their training will be framed as a multi-player differentiable game, optimized via simultaneous gradient descent. A rigorously defined consensus penalty, applied to a designated subset of shared parameters (e.g., lower-layer embeddings), will be added to each agent's task-specific loss, forcing their updates to converge. We will analyze the dynamics and stability of this process, drawing on concepts from optimization theory and generalization under distribution shift.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, game-theoretic framework for multi-agent NLP system co-design. It will yield empirical evidence on the emergence of more robust linguistic features and a theoretical analysis of consensus convergence in a deep learning setting. The resulting methodology moves beyond static multi-task learning towards a dynamic, coordinated ecosystem of models.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely fuses the formal machinery of consensus optimization (common in distributed computing) with the training of modern neural NLP agents, treating task specialization and shared representation learning as a coordinated game rather than a monolithic optimization problem.

proposals.title: Regret-Bounded Online Learning for Stable Semantic Drift in Dynamic Word Embeddings
proposals.motivation: Modern contextual language models (e.g., BERT, GPT) generate dynamic word representations, but their sequential adaptation within a discourse lacks theoretical guarantees. This can lead to unstable semantic drift, where a word's meaning shifts erratically, harming model robustness and interpretability. A principled framework is needed to bound this drift and ensure efficient, predictable adaptation to new contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: Framing the sequential update of a contextual representation generator as an online convex optimization problem against an adversarial loss will yield sublinear regret bounds, guaranteeing that the generator's performance is competitive with a hindsight-optimal policy and leading to more stable and efficient learning of dynamic meanings.
proposals.method: We will model a generator network (e.g., a lightweight Transformer module) that, at each step in a sequence, predicts the next contextual embedding for a word. Its updates will be governed by an online mirror descent algorithm, where the loss is provided by a discriminator or a downstream task, acting as the adversary. We will derive regret bounds for this sequential process by analyzing the convexity of the loss in the generator's parameters and the geometry of the representation space. The theoretical framework will be validated by training on dialogue and long-form text, measuring semantic drift stability and downstream task performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first regret-based theoretical guarantees for the online training of dynamic contextual representations, linking online learning theory to representation learning. It will yield a practical training algorithm that controls semantic drift, improving model robustness to covariate shift within documents and enabling more efficient adaptation in streaming data settings.
proposals.novelty: While online learning is well-studied, its direct application to bound the sequential regret of a *representation generator*—rather than a classifier—is novel. This bridges the gap between theoretical online convex optimization and the empirical practice of training dynamic embedding models.

proposals.title: The Inverse Discriminator Problem: A Primal Analysis of Optimal Discrimination Under a Fixed Generator
proposals.motivation: In standard GAN training, the joint dynamics of the generator and discriminator are complex and often unstable, making it difficult to isolate fundamental learning principles. By fixing the generator, we can study the isolated problem of optimal discriminator training, a crucial sub-problem for understanding generalization, optimization, and distribution matching in adversarial frameworks. This directly connects to Bottou's themes of training, distribution, and bias, providing a controlled setting to analyze how discriminators adapt to covariate shift between real and fixed generated data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that, for a fixed non-optimal generator, the optimally trained discriminator's loss surface and generalization gap are governed by the spectral properties of the data distributions, and that this optimal discriminator reveals a principled, model-based measure of the generator's shortcomings.
proposals.method: We will formulate the inverse discriminator problem as a standalone, convex (for common losses like logistic) optimization problem over the discriminator parameters, given a fixed generator G. Using tools from spectral analysis and generalization theory (inspired by 'Out of the Ordinary'), we will characterize the solution's dependence on the discrepancy between real and generated distributions. We will analyze the optimization landscape and generalization error, drawing connections to the 'memory viewpoint' of representation learning, and empirically validate the theory on synthetic and image datasets using standard architectures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, simplified theoretical framework for understanding discriminator behavior, isolating its role in distribution comparison. It will yield new insights into generalization under covariate shift in adversarial settings and could lead to more stable training diagnostics or novel objective functions for generator training informed by the optimal discriminator analysis.
proposals.novelty: While prior work studies discriminator gradients for generator updates, a systematic, optimization-centric study of the discriminator's *optimal state* under a fixed generator is largely unexplored. The novelty lies in treating the discriminator not as a tool, but as the primary object of study to derive distributional insights.

proposals.title: Data-Efficient Pre-training via Adversarial Corpus Synthesis and Curation
proposals.motivation: Modern self-supervised learning (SSL) for NLP and vision relies on massive, often proprietary, unlabeled corpora, creating a significant barrier to entry and research. In data-scarce domains or for smaller organizations, this limits the ability to learn broadly useful representations. This work addresses the core challenge of covariate shift between small, available data and the ideal training distribution required for robust generalization.
proposals.hypothesis: A high-quality, diverse synthetic corpus, generated by a fixed public foundation model and strategically curated, can serve as an effective proxy for large-scale natural data in SSL pre-training, enabling data-efficient learning of representations that generalize well to downstream tasks.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage framework. First, a generator (a frozen, publicly available large language model or image generator) produces synthetic data using targeted prompts or seeds derived from a small, authentic domain sample. Second, we curate this raw synthetic stream using an adversarial discriminator trained to distinguish high-quality, diverse examples, inspired by principles of active learning and distribution matching. The curated corpus then trains a target model via standard SSL objectives like masked language modeling or contrastive learning.
proposals.contribution: This work provides a practical, methodical pathway for effective pre-training in resource-constrained settings. It formally connects data generation, curation, and SSL under a distributional optimization framework, offering new insights into the bias-variance trade-offs of learning from synthetic data. The resulting public methodology lowers the barrier to developing capable foundation models.
proposals.novelty: While using models for data augmentation is known, the systematic synthesis and adversarial curation of an entire pre-training corpus as a substitute for missing large-scale data is novel. The approach explicitly treats the lack of data as an optimization problem over proxy distributions, aligning with Bottou's themes of training under distribution shift.

proposals.title: Constraint-Satisfying Generative Models for Data-Efficient Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Modern large-scale representation learning, particularly in NLP, relies on massive passive corpora, which are unavailable for many specialized domains and languages. This creates a data bottleneck and can embed societal biases present in the corpus. Drawing an analogy from structural biology's use of physics-based simulations under constraints, we propose a paradigm shift from passive prediction to active, rule-governed synthesis to build robust representations from limited data.
proposals.hypothesis: A model trained to generate synthetic data that must satisfy a set of explicit linguistic, logical, or causal constraints will develop more generalizable, compositional, and less biased representations than one trained solely on next-token prediction over a comparably sized, unconstrained corpus.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where a generative model (e.g., a Transformer decoder) is trained via a combined objective. First, a reconstruction loss on a small seed dataset of valid examples. Second, and primarily, a constraint satisfaction loss penalizing generated outputs that violate a provided set of rules. These rules could be implemented as differentiable functions (e.g., enforcing syntactic parse-tree consistency via a pretrained parser's gradients), logical constraints via semantic loss, or ontology-based relational graphs. Training will involve alternating between generating candidate outputs and updating model parameters to minimize constraint violation, akin to an energy-based model with the constraints defining the energy function.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for data-efficient and domain-adaptive representation learning, reducing dependency on large-scale data collection. It will offer a principled method to inject prior knowledge and control bias by design through the constraint set, directly addressing themes of distribution shift and generalization from Bottou's prior work.
proposals.novelty: While self-supervised learning uses implicit constraints from data, we propose making explicit, user-defined constraints the primary learning signal. This moves beyond data augmentation or pre-training by treating the model as a solver for a constraint satisfaction problem defined over the space of its own generations.

proposals.title: Active Self-Supervised Learning for Data-Efficient Representation Learning Under Covariate Shift
proposals.motivation: Modern self-supervised learning (SSL) assumes access to vast, uncurated unlabeled corpora, which is unavailable in specialized domains (e.g., medical imaging, scientific data) or under budget constraints. This creates a significant barrier to entry and may induce bias if the available large corpus does not match the target task's distribution. We need principled methods to learn effective representations when unlabeled data is scarce and potentially mismatched.
proposals.hypothesis: By actively selecting a minimal set of informative unlabeled data points based on their relationship to a small seed of labeled target data, we can train a model that learns representations robust to covariate shift, outperforming models trained on passively collected large corpora.
proposals.method: We will develop an active self-supervised learning framework. Starting from a small labeled seed from the target distribution, we will use an acquisition function—inspired by recent work on spectral adaptation for regression—to query the most informative unlabeled samples from a limited, potentially biased pool. These selected samples will be used to construct contrastive or relational pretext tasks, enforcing invariance to nuisance variations while preserving task-relevant features. The optimization will explicitly minimize a generalization bound for covariate shift, linking data selection to representation quality.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical algorithm for data-efficient representation learning in low-resource settings. It will theoretically connect active learning, covariate shift adaptation, and self-supervised objectives. The outcome will be a method that reduces dependency on massive unlabeled datasets while improving robustness to distribution mismatch.
proposals.novelty: While active learning typically queries for labels, we actively query for *unlabeled* data to construct better self-supervised tasks. Furthermore, we integrate spectral adaptation theory directly into the representation learning objective, a departure from post-hoc adaptation of pre-trained models.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning Linguistic Priors for Robust Generalization Under Distribution Shift
proposals.motivation: Modern NLP models often fail to generalize under covariate shift, as their learned representations can be brittle and overly tied to superficial training statistics. Infusing fixed linguistic knowledge can improve robustness but risks biasing models away from discovering novel, task-optimal representations. A method is needed to make linguistic knowledge a flexible, learnable scaffold that guides but does not rigidly constrain optimization, aligning with Bottou's focus on training dynamics and generalization across distributions.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learner that generates linguistically-informed initial parameters and adaptive regularization schedules will enable a primary model to achieve superior and more robust generalization by efficiently leveraging structured priors where beneficial while retaining the capacity to deviate from them based on empirical gradient signals.
proposals.method: We will implement a model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML-like) framework where the meta-learner's objective is to produce a parameter initialization and a per-layer regularization schedule from a bank of linguistic feature modules (e.g., syntactic dependency trees, morphological analyzers). The primary model (a Transformer) is then fine-tuned on a target task. The key innovation is an adversarial regularization module that dynamically adjusts the strength of the linguistic prior for each parameter group based on its alignment with the empirical gradient, allowing for pruning or strengthening of the scaffold. Training will use a bi-level optimization loop on a mixture of in-distribution and shifted datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodology for integrating structured knowledge into deep learning in a non-destructive, optimization-aware manner. It will yield new insights into the interaction between explicit priors and gradient-based learning, contributing to the theory of generalization under distribution shift. The resulting models are expected to show improved sample efficiency and robustness on linguistically complex tasks.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on fixed architectural infusions or static regularization, this approach treats linguistic knowledge as a dynamic, learnable resource managed by a meta-optimizer, creating an adaptive scaffold that can be modified by the primary training process.

proposals.title: Generative Grammar Networks: Validating Linguistic Priors Through Differentiable Production
proposals.motivation: Modern large language models learn linguistic structure implicitly from massive data, making them data-inefficient and difficult to interpret. This opacity raises concerns about generalization under distributional shifts and the entrenchment of social biases learned from corpora. A principled integration of explicit linguistic knowledge could improve sample efficiency, robustness, and provide a testable interface between symbolic and subsymbolic AI.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural generator, whose architecture and initial parameters are structurally anchored to explicit linguistic rules, will achieve comparable fluency to standard models with significantly less data, and the process of training for generation will provide a functional, gradient-based mechanism to validate and refine those initial linguistic priors.
proposals.method: We will design a Transformer-based conditional generator where specific attention heads and feed-forward layers are initialized and hardwired to compute linguistically meaningful functions (e.g., dependency parsing, agreement checking, semantic role labeling). These 'anchor modules' will be implemented as differentiable, parameterized circuits derived from formal grammars. The model will be trained on a standard language modeling objective, but with auxiliary losses that penalize violations of the anchored rules. The key experiment is to observe how the anchored modules adapt their parameters during training, measuring the divergence from their initial symbolic specification.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a novel architecture that fuses symbolic linguistic knowledge with neural generative models, providing a concrete framework for data-efficient and interpretable language generation. It will also introduce a new methodology—using generation as a pressure test—for empirically validating and refining linguistic theories in a subsymbolic system.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the standard paradigm: instead of extracting linguistic rules from a trained model, we inject and then functionally validate them through generation. This moves beyond simple regularization or data augmentation by making the linguistic structure a core, trainable component of the network's computational graph.

proposals.title: Structured Priors for Generalization: Infusing Linguistic Features into Transformer Initialization
proposals.motivation: Modern deep learning, particularly in NLP, often treats neural networks as tabula rasa, learning everything from data. This disregards decades of linguistic knowledge, potentially leading to inefficient training, poor generalization under covariate shift, and biases learned from finite datasets. Grounding networks in known structural priors could improve sample efficiency and robustness, aligning with Bottou's themes of learning, distribution, and bias.
proposals.hypothesis: That systematically initializing a transformer's attention and feed-forward layers to encode basic linguistic features (e.g., syntactic dependencies, semantic roles, morphological patterns) will lead to faster convergence, better data efficiency, and improved out-of-distribution generalization compared to standard random initialization.
proposals.method: We will design a library of differentiable, parameterized functions that map input token representations to outputs reflecting specific linguistic relationships. These functions will be used to construct the initial weight matrices of a transformer's key, query, and value projections, as well as its first feed-forward layer, setting the network's starting state to a 'linguistically aware' configuration. Training will then proceed normally on downstream tasks (e.g., GLUE, a shift benchmark), with the degree of prior infusion controlled and annealed. We will analyze the resultant learning dynamics and generalization gap, inspired by spectral adaptation methods for covariate shift.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete framework for integrating symbolic prior knowledge into modern neural architectures, moving beyond simple pre-training. It will yield new insights into the interaction between architectural bias and learned representations, and offer a path toward more sample-efficient and robust NLP models.
proposals.novelty: While pretraining on text encodes linguistic patterns statistically, we propose a direct, deterministic architectural infusion at initialization. This approach is distinct from auxiliary losses or data augmentation and directly addresses the 'disregard for prior knowledge' by hard-coding it into the model's starting point before any gradient steps.

proposals.title: Task-Agnostic Learning via Composable Output Primitives and Dynamic Routing
proposals.motivation: Current NLP models rely on task-specific output heads, which hinders efficient multi-task learning and generalization under distribution shift. This creates a mismatch between a shared backbone's rich representations and the rigid, task-specific output layers, limiting parameter sharing and adaptability. A unified, flexible output mechanism is needed to enable a single model to seamlessly handle diverse task structures without sacrificing performance.
proposals.hypothesis: A minimal set of differentiable output primitives, dynamically assembled by a lightweight router conditioned on a task descriptor, can achieve performance parity with dedicated task heads while significantly improving parameter efficiency and generalization across tasks with different output structures.
proposals.method: We will define a small vocabulary of core primitives (e.g., token/span classifiers, pointer networks, sequence generators). A shared Transformer backbone (e.g., BERT or T5) will produce contextual embeddings. A small, trainable adapter network will take a task embedding and dynamically generate routing weights and composition instructions. These will assemble the relevant primitives into the required output structure (e.g., for SQuAD: span selector for answer, token classifier for start/end). Training will use a multi-task objective, optimizing both the backbone, primitives, and router, with evaluation on benchmarks covering classification, QA, and generation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel architectural framework for truly general-purpose NLP models, reducing task-specific parameters and enabling efficient adaptation to new tasks. It will offer new insights into the relationship between output structure design and generalization, directly addressing themes of learning and distribution shift. The resulting models will be more parameter-efficient and easier to extend to novel tasks.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior multi-task approaches with fixed heads or simple projections, this method introduces a dynamic, compositional output layer. The key innovation is the formulation of output structure as a runtime assembly of minimal, shared primitives guided by a learned router, moving beyond static architectural commitments.

proposals.title: Universal Neural Interface: A Task-Agnostic Schema for NLP via Structured Output Learning
proposals.motivation: Current NLP systems require task-specific output heads or fine-tuning, creating a proliferation of model interfaces that hinder generalization and efficient adaptation to new tasks. This mirrors the pre-USB hardware problem of incompatible ports. A universal output interface could decouple core representation learning from task-specific decoding, enabling a single model to serve multiple applications without architectural changes, directly addressing distribution shift and bias in task adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: A single, structured, task-agnostic output schema—analogous to a universal data packet—can be learned by a core model, with lightweight, post-hoc 'driver' modules successfully interpreting this schema for diverse downstream tasks, thereby separating invariant representation from task-specific bias.
proposals.method: We will define a universal schema as a fixed-size, hierarchical key-value structure (e.g., a JSON-like template) representing core semantic constructs (entities, relations, attributes, events). A Transformer-based core model will be trained via multi-task learning on a mixture of supervised tasks (e.g., QA, classification, summarization) to populate this schema from input text, using a loss on the schema fields. Small, task-specific driver modules (e.g., shallow MLPs or rule-based parsers) will be trained separately to map the filled schema to final task formats (labels, text). We will analyze generalization under covariate shift by evaluating on out-of-distribution data for held-out tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will propose a concrete architecture for a universal NLP model interface, reducing the need for task-specific fine-tuning. It will provide a framework for analyzing task generalization and distributional robustness by isolating the core model's representations from task-specific biases, contributing to the theory of learning under distribution shift.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task learning exists, the explicit decoupling via a rigid, universal output schema and post-hoc drivers is novel, drawing a direct engineering analogy to hardware interfacing. This approach fundamentally re-frames task adaptation as a protocol interpretation problem rather than a parameter optimization one.

proposals.title: Generative Task Heads: A Unified Adversarial Framework for Multi-Task Output Spaces
proposals.motivation: Current multi-task learning architectures rely on distinct, task-specific output heads (e.g., linear layers for classification, decoders for text), which fail to capture the shared, complex geometry of high-dimensional, structured outputs like text or parse trees. This fragmentation limits generalization and robustness, especially under covariate shift, as it assumes outputs lie on simple, continuous manifolds. A unified, generative approach to the output layer could better model the true, discontinuous distribution of natural language data across tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: A single, conditionally-trained generative adversarial network (GAN), acting as a universal output layer, can map a shared model representation to the diverse, structured output spaces of multiple NLP tasks without mode collapse, outperforming discriminative, task-specific heads in robustness and data efficiency.
proposals.method: We will design a conditional GAN where the generator takes a shared Transformer encoder's representation and a task embedding to produce raw outputs (e.g., token sequences, tree structures). The discriminator will be tasked with distinguishing real task outputs from generated ones, conditioned on the same representation and task ID. We will adapt spectral normalization and employ techniques from recent analyses of GANs in high-dimensional spaces to stabilize training. Evaluation will involve a multi-task NLP benchmark, comparing against standard task-specific heads on in-distribution accuracy and out-of-distribution robustness under covariate shift.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel architectural paradigm that re-conceives multi-task output as a conditional generative process, unifying diverse task formats. It will yield new insights into the geometry of learned representations across tasks and demonstrate improved robustness to distributional shift by directly modeling the complex output data distribution.
proposals.novelty: The proposal is non-obvious because it directly challenges the standard practice of using simple discriminative projections for classification and structured outputs, instead positing that a single adversarial generator can handle this complexity. It uniquely fuses the multi-task representation learning theme with a rigorous, GAN-based analysis of high-dimensional, non-manifold output spaces.

proposals.title: Unified Task-Agnostic Decoding: A Single Output Layer for Multi-Task NLP via Distributional Alignment
proposals.motivation: Current NLP systems rely on task-specific output heads (e.g., classifiers, span selectors, generators), which limits flexibility, complicates multi-task learning, and introduces inductive bias. This fragmentation is at odds with the goal of building general-purpose models that can seamlessly adapt to new tasks without architectural changes. A unified output format would simplify system design and better test the hypothesis that task-specific knowledge resides primarily in the model's representations, not its output projection.
proposals.hypothesis: A single, fixed-dimensional output layer, trained with a distributional alignment objective across diverse tasks, can match or exceed the performance of specialized output heads by compelling the model to learn a truly task-agnostic semantic representation space.
proposals.method: We propose a Transformer encoder-decoder model where the decoder is a single, fixed linear projection layer. The model is trained on a mixture of NLP tasks (classification, QA, NLI) reformulated into a unified 'token-prediction' format: each task's target is encoded as a fixed-length sequence of discrete tokens from a shared vocabulary (e.g., label words, span markers, textual answers). The core innovation is a training objective combining standard cross-entropy with a distributional consistency loss, inspired by covariate shift correction, that minimizes the divergence between the output distributions of different task types, forcing the layer to be general. Optimization will use a meta-learning schedule to balance tasks from different distributions.
proposals.contribution: This work would provide a concrete architecture and training protocol for task-agnostic NLP modeling, reducing system complexity. It would offer new empirical evidence on the separation of representation learning and task-specific computation. The findings could challenge the necessity of specialized output heads and inform the design of more general and sample-efficient learning systems.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task learning is well-studied, enforcing a single, fixed output projection across disparate task *formats* (e.g., classification and generation) is largely unexplored. The key novelty is the explicit distributional alignment objective to achieve this, moving beyond simple weight-sharing to actively shape the output space for maximal generality.

proposals.title: Efficient Long-Range Context via Learned Hierarchical Indexing
proposals.motivation: Current Transformer models are fundamentally limited by the quadratic cost of self-attention, forcing the use of fixed context windows that truncate long-range dependencies. This is a critical bottleneck for tasks requiring reasoning over entire documents, books, or codebases. The problem is one of efficient search and retrieval over a long sequence, analogous to challenges solved in genomics via compressed, queryable data structures like the FM-index.
proposals.hypothesis: A language model can learn to construct a dynamic, multi-resolution index over an input sequence, enabling efficient approximate retrieval of relevant long-range context for the current local state, thereby decoupling computational cost from sequence length for dependency resolution.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage architecture: an indexing network and a query network. The indexing network processes the full input sequence to build a hierarchical latent summary structure (e.g., a tree of learned embeddings at increasing levels of compression). The query network, operating on a local context window, generates a query vector to traverse this index, retrieving a small set of salient, non-local representations. These are integrated via a sparse, gated mechanism into the standard Transformer block. We will adapt techniques from self-supervised learning to train the index construction and query mechanisms jointly with the language modeling objective, ensuring the index captures semantically useful summaries.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel architectural paradigm for handling ultra-long contexts with sub-quadratic complexity, directly addressing the scaling limitation of attention. It will contribute a theory for learned, task-adaptive compression in sequence models and offer new experimental benchmarks for long-range dependency tasks beyond the reach of standard Transformers.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing methods like sparse attention or memory networks, this approach draws a direct analogy to bioinformatics indexing, proposing that the model learns to build and query a compressed, searchable data structure of its own input, a form of dynamic, internal information retrieval.

proposals.title: Dynamic Contextual Binding: Learning Fluid Word Representations via Gated Memory Networks
proposals.motivation: Static word embeddings, while foundational, fail to capture the dynamic, context-dependent nature of meaning in natural language, leading to poor generalization under distributional shifts in discourse. This limitation is a core source of bias and error in language models, as they conflate distinct word senses into a single average representation. Addressing this aligns with the fundamental challenge of learning from non-i.i.d. data streams where the meaning of a symbol depends on its temporal and semantic context.
proposals.hypothesis: Word representations can be made more accurate and generalizable by dynamically modulating a core embedding with a gated memory of the ongoing discourse, allowing the model to learn context-specific bindings akin to working memory mechanisms in cognitive systems.
proposals.method: We propose a neural architecture where each word is represented by a base vector and a dynamic context vector. The context vector is produced by a gated attention mechanism over a fixed-size external memory module that stores recent hidden states of the discourse. Inspired by neuroscience models of binding and recent memory-augmented networks, a gating network, conditioned on the current input and memory state, controls the integration of context into the base representation. This dynamic representation serves as input to a downstream task model (e.g., language modeling, coreference resolution), with the entire system trained end-to-end using stochastic gradient descent and backpropagation through time.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel architecture for learning context-fluid word representations, directly addressing the limitations of static embeddings. It will contribute a new perspective on generalization in NLP by framing it as a problem of dynamic representation binding under covariate shift within a discourse. The results will offer insights into the interaction between memory, attention, and representation learning.
proposals.novelty: While contextual models like ELMo and BERT exist, our approach explicitly models the continuous, gated integration of discourse context into a separable core representation, drawing a direct analogy to computational neuroscience models of working memory binding. This offers a more interpretable and controlled mechanism for context-dependence compared to the deep, monolithic contextualization of large transformers.

proposals.title: Architectural Adaptation via Task-Structure Priors: A Unified Framework for Structured Prediction
proposals.motivation: Current NLP systems rely on bespoke architectures for different structured prediction tasks (e.g., CRFs for sequences, graph networks for parsing), creating a fragmented landscape that hinders systematic generalization analysis. This fragmentation makes it difficult to understand how optimization and learning dynamics are affected by the underlying output structure, a core theme in Bottou's work on training distributions and bias. A unified framework that adapts a core model to diverse structural constraints is needed to study learning principles across tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: A single transformer-based architecture, procedurally adapted via a meta-model that encodes formal task-structure priors (e.g., hard attention masks, pointer networks, or hierarchical sampling), can achieve performance competitive with specialized state-of-the-art models across a spectrum of structured NLP tasks, without task-specific architectural redesign.
proposals.method: We will first formalize a taxonomy of output structures (sequences, trees, DAGs, sets) and their associated constraints (autoregressive, hierarchical, permutation-invariant). A lightweight meta-model, inspired by hypernetwork and neural architecture search principles, will analyze a task's formal specification to configure a shared core Transformer. This configuration involves injecting structured sparsity patterns into attention, activating specialized output heads (e.g., for pointer mechanisms), or imposing layer stacking regimes. We will validate on a curated benchmark including semantic parsing (tree output), abstractive summarization (sequence compression), and mathematical reasoning (graph-structured derivation).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, code-level framework for rapidly instantiating models for new structured tasks, reducing the need for manual architecture engineering. It will yield new insights into how architectural inductive biases interact with optimization and generalization under different output distributions, directly extending research on covariate shift and learning dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses Transformers for multiple tasks, our approach systematically derives architectural adaptations from a formal task specification, making the structural bias explicit and configurable. This moves beyond simple fine-tuning or prompt engineering to a principled, generative process for model architecture based on computational constraints.

proposals.title: Saturation-Aware Multi-Task Learning: A Self-Regulating Mechanism for Unified NLP Architectures
proposals.motivation: Scaling unified neural architectures to a broad spectrum of NLP tasks (e.g., summarization, QA, translation) is hindered by training instabilities like catastrophic forgetting and imbalanced gradient flow. Pre-2026, multi-task and continual learning methods often rely on static loss weighting or complex regularization, which lack dynamic adaptation to the model's internal learning state. A principled, online method to detect and mitigate these instabilities is needed for robust, scalable systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The saturation of a model's text generation component—quantified by the plateauing of output token distribution entropy—serves as a reliable, real-time signal of localized optimization stagnation. This signal can be leveraged to dynamically re-weight task losses or modulate gradient pathways, thereby stabilizing training and improving generalization across a non-stationary task distribution.
proposals.method: We will construct a unified encoder-decoder Transformer backbone, integrating a spectrally adapted regression head for discriminative tasks alongside the standard autoregressive generator. During training, we will continuously monitor the entropy of the generator's output distribution per batch. Upon detecting a saturation event (entropy change below a threshold), we will employ an active learning-inspired strategy: the gradient from the saturated task's loss will be attenuated, while gradients from other, non-saturated tasks are amplified via a learned weighting network. This creates a feedback loop where the model's own learning dynamics guide resource allocation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel training algorithm that turns a common instability (saturation) into a built-in regulatory signal. It will advance the theory of optimization under covariate and task shift by linking local entropy dynamics to global learning stability. Practically, it will enable more robust and parameter-efficient multi-task NLP models.
proposals.novelty: While saturation is often viewed as a problem to be avoided, we propose to actively monitor and exploit it as a training signal. This differs fundamentally from pre-2026 methods like gradient surgery or fixed task scheduling, as the control mechanism is derived directly from the model's instantaneous generative behavior.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning to Dynamically Query Linguistic Structure for Robust Generalization
proposals.motivation: End-to-end neural models often lack robustness under distributional shifts, failing to leverage structured linguistic knowledge when it is most needed. Conversely, models with rigid symbolic priors can become brittle when that knowledge is incomplete or misleading. This creates a fundamental tension between adaptability and grounding, limiting reliable generalization in natural language processing.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural system that learns to conditionally activate and adapt relevant linguistic knowledge from a flexible resource, based on the input and its own internal state, will achieve superior robustness and generalization compared to models with fixed inductive biases or no explicit linguistic structure.
proposals.method: We propose a novel architecture with a controller network that meta-learns (via MAML or Reptile) to generate queries for a dynamic, graph-structured linguistic resource (e.g., ConceptNet, AMR). The controller's queries are conditioned on the input and the hidden states of a primary Transformer-based task model. The retrieved subgraph is adaptively integrated via a gated attention mechanism, allowing the model to modulate its influence. Training will employ a distributionally robust optimization (DRO) objective across multiple domains to force the controller to learn effective querying strategies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new framework for integrating flexible, structured knowledge into neural models, moving beyond static priors. It will yield empirical evidence on when and how linguistic structure aids generalization under covariate shift, contributing to the theory of hybrid systems.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the meta-learned controller that dynamically manages knowledge retrieval as a function of model state, rather than using knowledge as a fixed input feature or architectural constraint. This turns linguistic structure into a conditionally activated resource.

proposals.title: Hard-Coded Linguistic Manifolds: Guaranteeing Structural Faithfulness in Neural Language Models
proposals.motivation: Modern neural language models learn statistical patterns from data but often fail to consistently adhere to fundamental linguistic principles, such as hierarchical syntax or compositional semantics, leading to brittle generalization and uninterpretable failures. This is a core problem of distributional learning where the training objective does not enforce the correct inductive bias, resulting in models that exploit superficial correlations rather than capturing the true underlying structure. Ensuring models respect known linguistic constraints is crucial for robustness, interpretability, and reliable deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: By pre-wiring a neural network's computational graph and representational geometry to explicitly encode a priori linguistic structures (e.g., syntactic trees), we can constrain the learning process to a linguistically grounded manifold, guaranteeing that the final model adheres to these principles while still learning data-driven statistical refinements.
proposals.method: We will design novel neural modules where connectivity and weight-sharing patterns are deterministically set to mirror specific linguistic formalisms. For syntax, we will implement a Transformer variant where attention heads are initially hard-masked to enforce a dependency or constituency tree topology, with learnable parameters only scaling these fixed pathways. For semantics, we will construct geometric constraints in embedding space to reflect semantic frames or role-filler bindings. Training will involve standard gradient-based optimization, but the loss landscape will be intrinsically shaped by these immutable architectural priors, evaluated under covariate shift to test generalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework for infusing non-dilutable structural biases into neural architectures, moving beyond auxiliary regularization. It will yield models whose internal operations are inherently interpretable and guaranteed to respect specified linguistic rules, offering new insights into the interaction between hard constraints and gradient-based learning. The approach aims to improve sample efficiency and generalization by reducing the hypothesis space to linguistically plausible functions.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that adds linguistic knowledge as soft constraints or auxiliary tasks, this method inverts the paradigm by making the core architecture a direct, non-learnable embodiment of the prior, ensuring the bias cannot be overridden during training. This represents a shift from learning structure to learning within a pre-specified structural manifold.

proposals.title: Structured Linguistic Priors for Distributionally Robust Language Modeling
proposals.motivation: Modern large language models (LLMs) are trained on massive, heterogeneous corpora, leading to models that capture surface-level statistical regularities but often lack robust, compositional linguistic understanding. This can result in poor generalization under distribution shift, such as when syntactic structures or semantic compositions differ from the training distribution. Grounding neural architectures in established linguistic knowledge offers a path to more sample-efficient and systematically generalizable models.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating explicit, structured linguistic priors—specifically syntactic dependency trees and semantic role frames—into a Transformer's memory and attention mechanisms will improve its sample efficiency, compositional generalization, and robustness to covariate shifts in linguistic structure.
proposals.method: We will extend the 'memory viewpoint' of Transformers by designing a hybrid architecture where a symbolic linguistic parser (e.g., a transition-based dependency parser) operates in parallel with the standard self-attention layers. The parser's output will create a structured, persistent memory of syntactic and semantic relations. We will then bias the model's attention mechanism using a spectral adaptation technique, modulating attention scores based on the alignment between learned query-key vectors and the prior linguistic graph structure. This approach will be evaluated under active self-supervised learning regimes to measure data efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for integrating symbolic and sub-symbolic AI, moving beyond simple data augmentation. It will yield concrete metrics on how linguistic priors affect learning dynamics, sample complexity, and out-of-distribution robustness in language tasks, directly contributing to the theory of generalization under distribution shift.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has used linguistic features as input or auxiliary losses, our method directly modulates the core Transformer attention mechanism with a dynamically parsed, structured prior. This creates a continuous feedback loop between data-driven learning and symbolic constraints, a non-trivial integration at the architectural level.

proposals.title: Active Data Scaling: A Closed-Loop for Uncertainty-Guided Dataset Curation and Synthesis
proposals.motivation: The scaling laws for large language models show diminishing returns from simply adding more raw, uncurated data. This is inefficient and fails to address specific model weaknesses, such as systematic failures on certain reasoning patterns or under-represented data distributions. A data-centric approach, where the model guides the acquisition of its own training data, is a promising but underexplored path to more efficient scaling and robust generalization.
proposals.hypothesis: A learning system that uses its own uncertainty and performance profiles to guide targeted data synthesis and curation can achieve superior generalization and sample efficiency compared to passive scaling on static datasets, by directly addressing its knowledge gaps and distributional weaknesses.
proposals.method: We propose a closed-loop system where a primary model (e.g., a Transformer) is trained iteratively. After each phase, we analyze its loss and predictive uncertainty to identify failure modes and regions of high epistemic uncertainty in the input space. Inspired by active and self-supervised learning principles, we then activate two pipelines: 1) A synthesis pipeline that uses high-fidelity chain-of-thought generation or counterfactual augmentation to create examples targeting these weaknesses. 2) A curation pipeline that selects new external data based on distributional similarity to the identified gaps, drawing on covariate shift adaptation techniques. The newly created/curated data is then used for the next training cycle.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework and empirical evidence for data-centric scaling laws, moving beyond the naive 'more data' paradigm. It will yield practical algorithms for uncertainty-guided data acquisition that improve sample efficiency and robustness to distribution shift, directly contributing to the themes of optimization and generalization under changing data distributions.
proposals.novelty: While active learning selects from a fixed pool, and data augmentation applies static transformations, this work integrates model uncertainty with dynamic data *synthesis* and targeted external *curation* into a single, iterative scaling loop. This creates a self-improving system where the data distribution evolves in direct response to the model's learning state.

proposals.title: Curriculum-Driven Scaling: Synthetic Priming and Adaptive Filtering for Efficient Large-Scale Language Model Pre-Training
proposals.motivation: The scaling of language models is increasingly bottlenecked by the quality and efficient utilization of training data, not just compute. Current methods treat vast, noisy text corpora uniformly, leading to suboptimal learning trajectories and diminishing returns from scale. This research addresses the core challenge of extracting maximal learning signal from ultra-large datasets, a problem directly tied to generalization, optimization, and managing distributional bias in training.
proposals.hypothesis: A two-stage pre-training paradigm—first priming a model on a massive, self-generated synthetic dataset to establish foundational complexity, then applying a learned, progressive filtering curriculum to a raw text corpus—will yield more sample-efficient learning and better final generalization than uniform training on the raw data alone.
proposals.method: We will first implement a self-improving loop (e.g., using a pre-trained base model to generate synthetic text, which is then used to train a new model, iteratively) to create a diverse synthetic pre-training dataset. Second, we will train a utility-prediction model, inspired by active and self-supervised learning principles, to score data points in the raw corpus. This scorer will drive a curriculum where early training uses broadly filtered data, and later stages iteratively refine the filter to select higher-utility, more complex examples, adapting the training distribution over time.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for data-centric scaling that decouples the acquisition of foundational knowledge from efficient real-data mining. It will yield new insights into curriculum learning for autoregressive models and offer practical algorithms for managing the next order-of-magnitude increase in pre-training data scale, directly impacting training efficiency and model performance.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the explicit analogy to astronomical data processing—synthetic simulations for priming plus adaptive filtering—applied to language model pre-training. This combines the concept of self-generated synthetic data for bootstrapping with a *learned*, dynamic curriculum filter, moving beyond static heuristics like quality filtering or fixed schedules.

proposals.title: Learning Stable World Models via Ultra-Long Sequence Training and Memory-Augmented Transformers
proposals.motivation: Current large language models are trained on short, shuffled text segments, which limits their ability to learn and maintain coherent, long-range causal and narrative structures. This is a critical bottleneck for tasks requiring deep reasoning and consistency over extended contexts, such as multi-step problem-solving or generating long-form narratives. Training on ultra-long, ordered sequences presents a fundamental challenge in optimization and generalization, forcing the model to internalize stable representations of entities and relationships.
proposals.hypothesis: Training a transformer-based model on billions of meticulously ordered, book-length sequences, using an architecture designed for extreme context, will force the development of internal representations that function as a coherent world model, leading to significantly improved long-range coherence and reasoning.
proposals.method: We will construct a dataset of billions of long, ordered documents (e.g., books, long articles). Architecturally, we will extend the Transformer with a differentiable memory system, inspired by the 'Birth of a Transformer' memory viewpoint, to efficiently store and retrieve information across millions of tokens. We will investigate spectrally adapted optimization techniques, informed by Bottou's work on covariate shift, to stabilize training across the vastly different distributions of local and global context. The training objective will combine next-token prediction with auxiliary self-supervised tasks that reinforce long-range dependency learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete methodology and empirical evidence for training models on ultra-long sequences, moving beyond the fixed-context-window paradigm. It will demonstrate how architectural memory and training on structured long-form data can lead to emergent world models, offering a new path toward improved generalization and reasoning in AI systems.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the synergistic focus on both the data curation strategy (billions of ordered long sequences) and a memory-augmented architecture specifically designed to exploit this data, moving beyond simply scaling context windows. This directly tests whether long-range structure in data, coupled with appropriate inductive biases, can induce coherent internal world models.

proposals.title: Beyond the Data Plateau: Scaling Laws, Distributional Robustness, and Active Curation for Extreme-Scale Self-Supervised Learning
proposals.motivation: The empirical success of large language models is predicated on scaling training data, yet the fundamental relationship between dataset size, composition, and downstream generalization remains poorly understood. As we push towards training on orders of magnitude more unlabeled text, critical questions arise about diminishing returns, inherent biases in web-scale corpora, and the computational inefficiency of naive scaling. This research addresses the core challenge of how to scale data intelligently to improve model robustness and capability, not just size.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scaling training data with active, distribution-aware curation—rather than passive aggregation—will lead to superlinear improvements in out-of-distribution generalization and sample efficiency, breaking the predicted power-law scaling limits for models trained on passively collected internet text.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework combining: 1) Empirical scaling law analysis on multi-terabyte text mixtures to model performance as a function of data quantity and diversity. 2) A spectrally-inspired distributional distance metric (extending 'Spectrally Adapting Regression') to identify and prioritize underrepresented data regions in the feature space of a teacher model. 3) An active self-supervised learning loop (inspired by 'Active Self-Supervised Learning') that uses these metrics to guide the cost-efficient collection or synthesis of data batches that maximally reduce estimated generalization error under covariate shift, focusing computational budget on informative examples.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled methodology for data scaling that moves beyond 'more is better,' yielding actionable insights for dataset construction. It will contribute new algorithms for distribution-aware active learning in self-supervised contexts and empirically test the limits of scaling with curated data. The outcome will be a framework to build more robust and efficient models with less wasted compute.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of spectral analysis of representation spaces with active learning principles for self-supervised data scaling, a departure from the passive scaling paradigm. It directly investigates whether strategic intervention in data selection can alter the fundamental scaling laws observed for large models.

proposals.title: Beyond Attention Windows: A Study of Inductive Bias and Distributional Robustness in Alternative Sequence Architectures
proposals.motivation: The dominance of the Transformer architecture, particularly its fixed attention window, imposes a specific inductive bias that may limit generalization under distribution shift. Understanding how architectural choices in sequence modeling affect learning dynamics and out-of-distribution performance is a core, unresolved problem in machine learning, aligning with Bottou's focus on training, optimization, and bias.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that neural layer designs based on structured, adaptive memory mechanisms—inspired by principles of self-supervised learning—can yield better trade-offs between in-distribution efficiency and covariate shift robustness compared to standard windowed or full-sentence attention.
proposals.method: We will design and empirically compare a suite of novel layer types: 1) Spectrally adapted convolutional layers that dynamically adjust filter banks based on input statistics, 2) Gated recurrent units with actively learned, sparse relational graphs between hidden states, and 3) Memory-augmented layers employing content-based retrieval with a small, persistent external memory. These will be evaluated on controlled sequence modeling benchmarks under systematic covariate shifts, measuring generalization gaps and training efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic framework for evaluating architectural inductive bias in relation to distributional robustness. It will contribute new, interpretable layer designs and offer empirical insights into how architectural choices fundamentally shape a model's sensitivity to shifts in data distribution.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in synthesizing concepts from spectral adaptation, active self-supervision, and memory mechanisms into core neural layer designs, moving beyond mere architectural hybridization to a principled study of bias and robustness.

proposals.title: Optimization and Generalization Under Covariate Shift with Hierarchical Dilated Causal Convolutions
proposals.motivation: Modern sequence models, particularly Transformers, excel at capturing long-range dependencies but are computationally intensive and data-hungry, raising concerns about optimization efficiency and generalization under distributional shift. Convolutional models offer parameter efficiency and strong local inductive biases but struggle with long-range context, limiting their robustness in tasks like language modeling where test distributions may differ from training. This work investigates a hybrid architecture designed to balance these trade-offs, directly addressing themes of efficient training and generalization across distributions.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical model combining dilated causal convolutions with localized, lightweight attention will achieve superior optimization efficiency and better generalization under covariate shift compared to standard Transformers, by leveraging the strong local feature learning of CNNs while explicitly modeling critical long-range dependencies.
proposals.method: We will design a stack of dilated causal convolutional blocks to build a multi-scale feature hierarchy over text. Each block's output features will be processed by a lightweight, localized self-attention head (e.g., a linear or factorized attention variant) that operates on a constrained window derived from the dilated receptive field. This design maintains causality and computational efficiency. We will rigorously evaluate optimization trajectories, parameter efficiency, and performance under synthetic and real-world covariate shifts, benchmarking against Transformer baselines and analyzing the learned feature distributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, efficient architecture for hierarchical text modeling with provable causal structure. It will yield new insights into the interaction between architectural inductive bias, optimization dynamics, and robustness to distribution shift, contributing to the development of more sample-efficient and reliable sequence models.
proposals.novelty: The integration is non-obvious because it inverts the standard paradigm: instead of adding convolutions to an attention-based model, it uses a convolutional feature hierarchy as the primary computational engine, augmenting it *selectively* with minimal attention to compensate for a known CNN weakness, creating a fundamentally different optimization and generalization profile.

proposals.title: Convolutional Feature Hierarchies for Text: A Framework for Efficient and Generalizable Sequence Modeling
proposals.motivation: The dominance of Transformer-based models for text has led to high computational costs and challenges in analyzing learned representations. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) offer a compelling alternative with inherent advantages in computational efficiency, hierarchical feature extraction, and strong inductive biases for local pattern recognition. Investigating their application to text sequences can address issues of training efficiency and distributional robustness, core themes in Bottou's research, by providing a simpler, more analyzable model of language structure.
proposals.hypothesis: That a carefully designed, deep convolutional network can learn hierarchical feature representations from text sequences that are competitive with Transformer-based models on core NLP tasks, while offering superior sample efficiency and robustness to covariate shift due to its strong spatial inductive biases.
proposals.method: We will design a deep, purely convolutional encoder architecture using 1D convolutions, gated linear units, and residual connections, inspired by pre-2026 models like ByteNet and ConvSeq2Seq. The model will process subword token embeddings, building a multi-scale representation via dilated convolutions and downsampling layers. We will train it using a combination of supervised objectives (e.g., classification, sequence tagging) and self-supervised objectives inspired by contrastive learning, aligning with the 'Active Self-Supervised Learning' theme, to learn general features. Evaluation will focus on generalization across shifted text distributions and comparative analysis of learned feature hierarchies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, modern benchmark for convolutional sequence models, demonstrating their viability as efficient alternatives to Transformers. It will contribute a new perspective on hierarchical feature learning in NLP and offer insights into the relationship between architectural bias, sample efficiency, and robustness to distributional change, directly extending research on covariate shift and generalization.
proposals.novelty: While CNNs for text are not new, a systematic pre-2026 investigation leveraging recent advances in deep convolutional architectures and self-supervised learning, framed explicitly through the lens of optimization, generalization, and distributional robustness, represents a significant and non-obvious synthesis of Bottou's core themes.

proposals.title: Generalization Under Covariate Shift via Long-Range Auxiliary Tasks in Recurrent Networks
proposals.motivation: Recurrent neural networks (RNNs) like LSTMs and GRUs are foundational for sequence modeling, but their ability to genuinely capture and utilize long-range dependencies is often overstated, relying on short-term patterns. This failure limits their robustness and generalization when transferred to downstream tasks, such as time-series forecasting, where the test distribution may differ from training (covariate shift) and long-term context is critical. A principled method to force RNNs to build hidden states that preserve information over unbounded spans is needed to improve out-of-distribution performance.
proposals.hypothesis: Training a gated recurrent network with a primary sequence modeling objective and a secondary, explicit long-range token/relation prediction auxiliary task will produce hidden state representations that are more robust to distributional shifts in downstream applications. This is because the auxiliary objective directly optimizes for information retention over large distances, mitigating the network's tendency to rely on local, spurious correlations.
proposals.method: We will implement a deep LSTM or GRU network trained with a composite loss: the standard next-token prediction cross-entropy plus an auxiliary loss. The auxiliary task will involve predicting a token or a relational label (e.g., sentence-level topic) that is separated from the current position by a large, randomly sampled distance (k), where k can span the entire sequence length. We will investigate spectral normalization techniques, inspired by pre-2026 covariate shift research, to stabilize training under this dual objective. The model's learned representations will be evaluated by fine-tuning on time-series datasets with documented temporal distribution shifts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel training framework for RNNs that explicitly enforces long-range contextual awareness, moving beyond architectural modifications. It will yield a rigorous evaluation protocol for representation robustness under covariate shift in sequential domains. The resulting models are expected to demonstrate superior transfer learning performance in time-series analysis tasks where long-term dependencies are key.
proposals.novelty: While auxiliary tasks are common, their use for explicitly enforcing unbounded context preservation in RNNs—as a direct intervention for improving generalization under distribution shift—is unexplored. This approach connects the learning dynamics of recurrent networks with the theoretical framework of generalization across different distributions, a core theme in Bottou's pre-2026 research.

proposals.title: Reservoir Computing for Language: A Fixed-Dynamics Approach to Unbounded Context Modeling
proposals.motivation: Modeling long-range dependencies in text sequences remains a core challenge in NLP, as conventional RNNs trained with backpropagation-through-time suffer from computational expense, vanishing gradients, and training instability over long contexts. Reservoir Computing (RC) offers a proven, efficient alternative for temporal modeling in dynamical systems, using a fixed, randomly initialized recurrent network to project inputs into a rich temporal feature space. This project investigates whether this principle can be transferred to NLP to capture textual context more efficiently and stably.
proposals.hypothesis: A large, fixed, randomly initialized recurrent neural network (the reservoir), driven by word embeddings, can generate a high-dimensional temporal state that linearly encodes sufficient information for accurate sequence prediction, thereby bypassing the need for costly gradient-based training of the recurrent core while capturing very long-range dependencies.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Echo State Network (ESN) framework to text. A large RNN with tanh activations and sparse random weights (the reservoir) will be constructed, ensuring the Echo State Property. Pre-trained static word embeddings (e.g., GloVe) will excite the reservoir over a text sequence. Only a simple linear readout layer, mapping the reservoir's state to a prediction target (e.g., next word via a softmax over vocabulary), will be trained using ridge regression or online least squares. We will experiment with reservoir dimensionality, spectral radius, and input scaling on language modeling benchmarks, comparing performance and context length against standard RNNs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, computationally efficient method for temporal modeling in NLP, potentially enabling the study of much longer textual contexts. It will establish a formal bridge between dynamical systems theory (via RC) and language processing, offering new insights into the nature of temporal representation. The results will contribute to the understanding of learning under distribution shift by analyzing how a fixed dynamical system generalizes across different textual distributions.
proposals.novelty: While RC is established in time-series analysis, its application to discrete symbolic sequences in NLP, with a focus on leveraging pre-2026 static word embeddings to drive the reservoir dynamics, is largely unexplored. The core novelty is the complete decoupling of complex temporal dynamics learning (handled by fixed, random recurrence) from task-specific readout learning, a radical departure from end-to-end trained RNNs or Transformers.

proposals.title: Saturation as a Signal: Guided Transfer of Recurrent Networks for Stable Long-Context Language Modeling
proposals.motivation: Modern language models struggle with unbounded context, while RNNs pre-trained on long-horizon time-series data possess strong inductive biases for temporal coherence. However, directly transferring these RNNs to text often leads to instability and catastrophic forgetting due to distribution shift. This work addresses the core challenge of robustly adapting temporal priors to linguistic structure, a critical step for models requiring stable long-range reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: The saturation dynamics of recurrent units during transfer learning are not merely a failure mode but an informative signal that can be used to dynamically guide the adaptation process, leading to more stable training and better preservation of useful long-context priors.
proposals.method: We will pre-train robust RNNs (e.g., Lipschitz RNNs, UnICORNN) on long-sequence time-series forecasting. During transfer to language modeling (e.g., WikiText), we will monitor per-unit saturation metrics (e.g., hidden state norms, activation derivatives). This signal will control a meta-controller that dynamically adjusts the training regime: freezing saturated layers, applying layer-wise learning rate modulation, or injecting targeted noise to escape poor minima. The controller's policy will be optimized using a multi-objective loss balancing language modeling loss and a stability regularizer based on saturation rate.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodology for stable cross-domain transfer of recurrent architectures, explicitly linking internal dynamical states to training stability. It will yield new insights into the relationship between saturation, forgetting, and generalization under covariate shift, contributing to the broader field of robust transfer learning.
proposals.novelty: Inverting the standard view of saturation as a purely negative phenomenon to repurpose it as a guiding signal for adaptive training is a non-obvious departure from existing work, which typically seeks to eliminate saturation entirely. This creates a direct synergy between network dynamics and curriculum learning.

proposals.title: Ensemble Diversity Under Covariate Shift: A Spectral and Memory-Driven Approach for Robust Generalization
proposals.motivation: A core challenge in machine learning is ensuring models generalize when test distributions differ from training (covariate shift). Standard ensemble methods often fail under shift because member models share similar inductive biases, leading to correlated errors. This work addresses the need for ensembles explicitly designed for distributional robustness, a key concern in reliable deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: An ensemble composed of neural network variants trained with distinct spectral regularization schemes and architectural biases (e.g., from a memory-augmented perspective) will exhibit greater functional diversity, leading to significantly improved and more robust generalization under covariate shift compared to standard ensembles.
proposals.method: We will develop an ensemble where each member is a model variant (e.g., standard feedforward, a simplified transformer block, a memory-augmented network) trained on the same data but with different spectral adaptation constraints inspired by spectrally adapting regression. A meta-learning layer will actively weight the ensemble members' predictions based on an online estimate of the local spectral properties of the test batch, effectively performing a low-cost, self-supervised adaptation to shift.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel framework for constructing robust ensembles by formally linking architectural bias, spectral regularization, and ensemble diversity.
